mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 7 Series 730d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW 7 Series 730d engine costs £3,500–£7,000 supply only, varying by generation: M57 £2,500–£4,500, M57TU2 £3,000–£5,000, N57 £4,000–£6,500, B57 £4,500–£7,500, G70 B57 MHEV £5,500–£9,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

That depends on which generation. The M57 is robust but ageing; the M57TU2 is refined and generally reliable; the N57 has a rear-mounted timing chain risk (pre-2015); the B57 (G11/G12/G70) is BMW's most advanced and reliable diesel six. The G11/G12 B57 is widely considered the sweet spot — powerful, efficient and exceptionally durable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 7 Series 730d?

acceptedAnswer.text: M57/M57TU2: injector failure (rough idle, misfire, £300–£800 per injector), DPF/EGR issues (£400–£1,200). N57: timing chain tensioner wear (cold-start rattle, £1,500–£3,500), injector failure, DPF/EGR. B57: cooling system leaks (£400–£1,000), DPF/EGR issues (less common). G70 B57: 48V MHEV system issues (£800–£2,500).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 7 Series 730d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: 7 Series 730d values range from £3,000–£70,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £3,500–£7,000 reconditioned engine represents 10–80% of vehicle value — our 70% BMW scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained G11/G12 or G70, replacement is almost always justified. For an ageing E38/E65 with other issues, assess scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 7 Series 730d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 730d has used five engine variants: M57D30 (E38, 1996–2001, 193bhp), M57TU2 (E65, 2002–2008, 231bhp), N57D30 (F01, 2009–2015, 245bhp), B57D30 (G11/G12, 2016–2022, 265bhp), and B57D30 MHEV (G70, 2023–present, 286bhp). Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N57 timing chain issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Timing chain and guide replacement costs £1,500–£3,500 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£4,000–£6,500). The job requires engine removal on the F01, making it a major investment. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — ignored chain wear can lead to catastrophic piston-valve contact.

eraMap.title: BMW 7 Series 730d — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: BMW's first 7 Series diesel — 193bhp, robust but aged
231bhp — refined, improved DPF, injectors need monitoring
245bhp — chain tensioner and DPF are key watchpoints
265bhp — modern B57, chain front-mounted, very robust
286bhp with MHEV — latest refinement, 48V complexity added

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across all 730d generations. Older generations (E38/E65) typically run lower on the used market. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 7 Series 730d engine typically takes 10–18 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent BMW specialist) or £120–£170/hr (London/SE dealer). The B57's 48V system adds complexity for G70 models.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 7 Series 730d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Forced regen or cleaning — worth it

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW 7 Series 730d? Check These First
buyIf: * G11/G12 or G70 (B57) — the most refined and reliable 730d generations
* Full service history with regular oil changes (8,000–10,000 miles)
* Documented injector history (M57/N57) or timing chain history (N57)
avoidIf: * F01 pre-2015 with no timing chain history — the risk is real
* Any evidence of DPF or EGR issues — warning lights, reduced power
* Short-journey-only history — DPF issues are almost certain
* Poor service history — all 730d engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 730d is BMW's quintessential luxury diesel — refined, effortless and capable of immense mileages. The E38 started it all, the E65 refined it, the F01 added power, and the G11/G12/G70 perfected it. The B57-powered G11/G12 and G70 are the most reliable, the N57 F01 has a chain risk (pre-2015), and the M57-era cars are robust but ageing. Buy the newest you can afford, maintain it properly, and the 730d will cover quarter-million miles with ease.

cta.label: → Compare BMW 7 Series 730d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 7 Series 730d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Any rattle? N57 chain rattle is the biggest warning; M57/B57 should be quiet
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — see the Era Map above
* Timing Chain History — N57: chains replaced? Tensioner checked? (pre-2015 is critical)
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; frequent short trips cause DPF issues
* EGR System — Any coolant loss or white smoke? EGR cooler failure is common
* Injector Performance — Check for smooth acceleration; injector issues are common on high-mileage cars
* 48V System (G70) — Any battery warnings? MHEV system checked?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
